clusters.title: Scotland becomes first part of UK to screen newborns for spinal muscular atrophy
clusters.neutral_headline: Scotland Screens Newborns For Spinal Muscular Atrophy

items.title: Scotland becomes first part of UK to screen newborns for spinal muscular atrophy
items.standfirst: Hope Scottish pilot will result in heel prick test for rare genetic condition being approved across BritainScotland has become the first part of the UK to screen newborn babies for spinal muscular atrophy (SMA), a rare genetic condition that causes progressive muscle wastage.Campaigners, who have long advocated for newborn testing because early detection is critical for treatment outcomes, hope the Scottish pilot will result in approval for the heel prick test across the UK. Continue reading...

Continue reading...

clusters.title: Weight-loss drugs do work – but not on their own | Letters
clusters.neutral_headline: Weight Loss Drugs Work With Lifestyle Adjustments

items.title: Weight-loss drugs do work – but not on their own | Letters
items.standfirst: GLP-1 medicines work best alongside lifestyle adjustments, writes Dr Sam Robson. Plus letters from Anne Williams, Siân Williams, and a reader who has had success with MounjaroYour editorial (15 March) is right to highlight the growing evidence that GLP-1 medicines influence the brain’s reward systems and may have potential in treating addiction. These drugs represent an important therapeutic advance, and the moralising that has historically surrounded obesity treatment is both unhelpful and scientifically outdated. However, in clinical practice it is also clear that medication alone is rarely the whole answer.Obesity, like addiction, involves powerful biological drivers such as appetite signalling, reward pathways and metabolic adaptation, but it also unfolds within behavioural and environmental contexts. Patients who achieve the most durable outcomes are typically those who combine pharmacological treatment with meaningful changes in diet quality, physical activity, sleep and muscle preservation. Continue reading...
items.content: GLP-1 medicines work best alongside lifestyle adjustments, writes Dr Sam Robson. Plus letters from Anne Williams, Siân Williams, and a reader who has had success with MounjaroYour editorial (15 March) is right to highlight the growing evidence that GLP-1 medicines influence the brain’s reward systems and may have potential in treating addiction. These drugs represent an important therapeutic advance, and the moralising that has historically surrounded obesity treatment is both unhelpful and scientifically outdated. However, in clinical practice it is also clear that medication alone is rarely the whole answer.Obesity, like addiction, involves powerful biological drivers such as appetite signalling, reward pathways and metabolic adaptation, but it also unfolds within behavioural and environmental contexts. Patients who achieve the most durable outcomes are typically those who combine pharmacological treatment with meaningful changes in diet quality, physical activity, sleep and muscle preservation. Continue reading...

ai_summary: Weight loss drugs are effective with lifestyle changes
GLP-1 medicines influence brain's reward systems
Obesity treatment must be comprehensive
Medication alone is not enough
Lifestyle adjustments are crucial for success

clusters.title: Trump and RFK Jr touted leucovorin as a treatment for autism. The FDA quietly walked it back
clusters.neutral_headline: FDA Walks Back Leucovorin Treatment For Autism

items.title: Trump and RFK Jr touted leucovorin as a treatment for autism. The FDA quietly walked it back
items.standfirst: Outpatient prescriptions for the drug increased 71% after the announcement, but the FDA later approved it only for folate deficiency When Donald Trump and Robert F Kennedy Jr stood up at the press conference in September to tout leucovorin, a vitamin B derivative, as a treatment for autism, some neurodevelopmental doctors were shocked – and they braced themselves. There was little evidence to suggest the folinic acid helps with autism, yet there was an immediate flood of parents calling and scheduling visits to talk about the medication.“The average parent who maybe wasn’t getting the right information said, ‘Well, to be good parents, we need to try this,’” said William Graf, a professor of pediatrics and neurology at Connecticut Children’s Medical Center. Outpatient leucovorin prescriptions for children ages five to 17 in the US rose by 71% in the weeks following the announcement, new research shows. Continue reading...
items.content: Outpatient prescriptions for the drug increased 71% after the announcement, but the FDA later approved it only for folate deficiency When Donald Trump and Robert F Kennedy Jr stood up at the press conference in September to tout leucovorin, a vitamin B derivative, as a treatment for autism, some neurodevelopmental doctors were shocked – and they braced themselves. There was little evidence to suggest the folinic acid helps with autism, yet there was an immediate flood of parents calling and scheduling visits to talk about the medication.“The average parent who maybe wasn’t getting the right information said, ‘Well, to be good parents, we need to try this,’” said William Graf, a professor of pediatrics and neurology at Connecticut Children’s Medical Center. Outpatient leucovorin prescriptions for children ages five to 17 in the US rose by 71% in the weeks following the announcement, new research shows. Continue reading...

ai_summary: FDA walks back leucovorin treatment for autism
Trump and RFK Jr touted the treatment
Little evidence supports leucovorin for autism
Outpatient prescriptions increased after announcement
FDA approved leucovorin for folate deficiency only

clusters.title: I have stage four cancer – there will be no cure, but death isn’t necessarily imminent: this is how it feels to live in the long middle

items.title: I have stage four cancer – there will be no cure, but death isn’t necessarily imminent: this is how it feels to live in the long middle
items.standfirst: When you are cured, the world cheers; when you are dying, it mourns. But when you are simply maintaining, the world is at a lossMornings begin with a silent inventory, conducted in the dark before the curtains are drawn: can I breathe easily today? The question is stripped of all poetic veneer. When you have stage four lung cancer, breath is no longer a background process; it is a finite currency I must spend with the caution of a miser. It dictates the architecture of my day, the borders of my energy and the very cadence of my speech.I am not a “survivor” in the triumphalist sense of the word, nor am I imminently dying. I occupy the long middle – a rarely charted territory where the body remains fragile, treatment constant, and life does not so much move forward as stubbornly persist. Continue reading...
items.content: When you are cured, the world cheers; when you are dying, it mourns. But when you are simply maintaining, the world is at a lossMornings begin with a silent inventory, conducted in the dark before the curtains are drawn: can I breathe easily today? The question is stripped of all poetic veneer. When you have stage four lung cancer, breath is no longer a background process; it is a finite currency I must spend with the caution of a miser. It dictates the architecture of my day, the borders of my energy and the very cadence of my speech.I am not a “survivor” in the triumphalist sense of the word, nor am I imminently dying. I occupy the long middle – a rarely charted territory where the body remains fragile, treatment constant, and life does not so much move forward as stubbornly persist. Continue reading...

ai_summary: Stage four lung cancer diagnosis

Treatment is constant and ongoing
Energy levels dictate daily activities

clusters.title: ‘Thank God they’re still alive’: Kaiser therapists claim its new screening system puts patients at higher risk by delaying their care
clusters.neutral_headline: ‘Thank God they’re still alive’: Kaiser therapists claim its new screening system puts patients at higher risk by delaying their care

items.title: ‘Thank God they’re still alive’: Kaiser therapists claim its new screening system puts patients at higher risk by delaying their care
items.standfirst: Kaiser pushed back on striking workers’ claims and AI fears, saying it delivers ‘timely, high-quality care to meet members’ needs’Ilana Marcucci-Morris is worried about the patients she treats and how long it took for them to arrive in her office. At Kaiser Permanente’s psychiatry outpatient clinic in Oakland, California, she says she increasingly finds herself assessing people experiencing more severe mental health issues than two years ago. For those who do make it to their appointments, she thinks: “Thank God they’re still alive.”It wasn’t always this way, according to Marcucci-Morris, a licensed clinical social worker. Licensed professionals used to almost always be the first point of contact for patients with behavioral health issues at Kaiser, she said. Had some of these patients received a screening with a licensed healthcare professional, she suspects they would have received an appointment within days instead of a meeting with her a week or two later. Continue reading...
items.content: Kaiser pushed back on striking workers’ claims and AI fears, saying it delivers ‘timely, high-quality care to meet members’ needs’Ilana Marcucci-Morris is worried about the patients she treats and how long it took for them to arrive in her office. At Kaiser Permanente’s psychiatry outpatient clinic in Oakland, California, she says she increasingly finds herself assessing people experiencing more severe mental health issues than two years ago. For those who do make it to their appointments, she thinks: “Thank God they’re still alive.”It wasn’t always this way, according to Marcucci-Morris, a licensed clinical social worker. Licensed professionals used to almost always be the first point of contact for patients with behavioral health issues at Kaiser, she said. Had some of these patients received a screening with a licensed healthcare professional, she suspects they would have received an appointment within days instead of a meeting with her a week or two later. Continue reading...

ai_summary: Kaiser Permanente's new screening system criticized
Therapists claim it delays patient care and increases risk
Patients experience more severe mental health issues
Licensed professionals no longer first point of contact
Screening system may cause appointments to be delayed
